title: Your First Systems Investigation: What Makes It Work?
description: Analyze a technological system you use every day by breaking it into inputs, processes, outputs, and feedback loops. This session builds systems thinking, digital literacy, and design awareness.

steps.instruction: Choose a technological system you use daily: a coffee maker, dishwasher, phone, or bicycle. Draw a simple diagram showing inputs, process, and outputs. Read about the IPO model: what counts as an input vs an output?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have an IPO diagram with at least 3 inputs (energy, materials, information, or human effort) and 2-3 outputs (including any waste or heat outputs)
steps.hint: Don't forget energy as an input and heat as an output — most systems lose energy as heat

steps.instruction: Does your system have any feedback? (A thermostat senses temperature and adjusts heating; a dishwasher has a timer.) Read about feedback and control: how does feedback change a system's behavior?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify whether the system uses feedback and explain how it makes the system more accurate, efficient, or responsive
steps.hint: A system without feedback runs open-loop — it cannot correct errors. Feedback enables self-regulation

steps.instruction: Think about the tools used to make your system. Were they hand tools, power tools, or automated machinery? Read about hand tools and power tools: what level of skill and precision does each require?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can describe the manufacturing process for one component of your system and match it to the appropriate tool/process type
steps.hint: Complex shaped parts are injection molded (automated); custom or one-off parts are machined or fabricated by hand

steps.instruction: If your system is digital (phone, computer), consider: how is information stored and processed? Read about binary and data representation: what does it mean that all digital information is 0s and 1s?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can explain that digital information is encoded as binary numbers and describe what a byte represents
steps.hint: A photo is millions of pixels, each stored as three numbers (RGB values) — a 12-megapixel photo contains ~36 million numbers stored in binary

steps.instruction: Identify one societal impact of your chosen technology — positive or negative. Read about technology and society: who benefits and who might be harmed by this technology?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can articulate a nuanced societal impact that includes both benefits and harms, and identify a group that experiences the technology differently from the majority
steps.hint: Every technology has distributional effects — some groups benefit more than others and some bear disproportionate costs
